1000 Essential Vocabulary — for Cihan

Curated B2 → C1 daily-life words Cihan doesn't already know, mapped to specific gaps in the diagnostic analysis. Click any word card to open a full pop-up with multiple examples, usage notes, and related words.

📚 1025 new words
🎯 CEFR B2 → C1
🔍 Click any word for full detail
🧪 Tests after every chunk
📖 Narrative story per chunk

Diagnostic Focus

Curated against diagnostics/cihan_analysis.md — every section targets a documented weakness.

★ Formal connectors
however, therefore, moreover, nevertheless, whereas — 0 hits in his transcripts.
★ C1 evaluative adjectives
substantial, significant, robust, plausible — replaces over-used 'good'.
★ C1 hedging
arguably, presumably, broadly, essentially — replaces over-used 'I think'.
★ Business register
stakeholder, deliverable, bandwidth, escalate, prioritise.
★ Native collocations
make a decision, throw a party, do exercise — replace Turkish calques.
★ Opinion frames
in my view, on balance, that being said — replace 'I think maybe'.
★ Phrasal verbs for work
sort out, follow up, kick off, wrap up.
Daily-life domain
errand, chore, leftover, commute — gaps from his B2 range.

Mini-story

Sunday morning, the flat was finally his to enjoy. The duvet still smelled of warm cotton, and a single shaft of light cut between the curtains. He padded across the rug, opened a drawer, took out the kettle from the shelf and filled the sink to do the breakfast washing-up. On the hallway side-table sat the dustbin he'd promised to empty for two days. He carried the recycling out, closed the wardrobe in the bedroom on his way back, and stood on the staircase for a moment listening to the building wake up. A quiet ceiling, a tidy flat, a kettle starting to boil — sometimes that was the whole victory of the morning.

Words from this section: cutlery, appliance, blanket, duvet, mattress, bedding, curtain, blinds…

peel (verb) — remove the outer skin

Mini-story

He preheated the oven and put a tray on the worktop. The recipe was simple: chop the onion, dice the garlic, slice the tomatoes thin. He peeled the carrots, grated a bit of cheese for later, and let a pan warm on the hob. Once the oil shimmered, he tipped in the onions and stirred. The sauce would simmer for twenty minutes, the chicken would roast for forty, and the broccoli he'd steam at the end so it stayed bright green. He whisked the dressing in a small bowl, seasoned everything with herbs and salt, and reached for the ladle as the soup started bubbling. The kitchen smelled like a proper Sunday.

Words from this section: chop, slice, dice, grate, peel, stir, whisk, simmer…

Mini-story

The farmers' market in November was a quiet pleasure. Fresh, ripe tomatoes sat next to stale-looking pumpkins; the dairy stall had cheeses he'd never tried, and the herb seller was tying small bundles of parsley and thyme. He picked a savoury sourdough loaf with a thick crust, a small portion of olives, and a tub of creamy yoghurt for breakfast. Nothing here was bland — every ingredient had a character. He resisted the dessert stall, paid, and walked home with cuisine for a whole week in his backpack: vegetarian for Monday, seafood for Wednesday, a proper roast for Sunday.

Words from this section: dairy, poultry, seafood, vegetarian, vegan, nutritious, crunchy, creamy…

Mini-story

Her alarm went off at six. She hit snooze once — only once — then forced herself out of bed to start her routine. A quick breakfast, then she rushed through the morning: laundry on, groceries list on the fridge, three small errands lined up. The commute through rush hour was its usual ordeal, but she made the train. At lunch she rescheduled a meeting, postponed another, and politely cancelled a third. By evening she'd ticked off every chore, tidied the kitchen, even started to declutter the bedside drawer. She didn't oversleep often, but tonight she would — gladly.

Words from this section: errand, chore, commute, rush hour, schedule, postpone, hurry, oversleep…

Mini-story

Saturday morning was for slowing down. He shaved carefully, rinsed his face, and reached for the sunscreen even though the sky was grey. A new toothbrush, a fresh tube of toothpaste, a drop of deodorant. He used conditioner for the first time in weeks and his hair felt better for it. He had a small blemish on his chin, but a dab of cream and it was forgotten. The barber's was at eleven — a trim and a haircut, maybe a small beard tidy-up. He packed his toiletries for the gym, took a moment to pamper himself with a cup of tea, and stepped out feeling almost luxurious.

Words from this section: hygiene, razor, shave, toothpaste, toothbrush, floss, rinse, moisturise…

Mini-story

The wardrobe interview was Monday, so on Sunday she laid out her smart outfit: a navy blazer in soft fabric, a crisp cotton shirt, a leather belt, dark trousers that didn't sit too tight or too loose. The jumper she'd worn at university was too scruffy for this. She checked the sleeves, the collar, the zip on her handbag. Trainers in the backpack, smart shoes on. She added a thin scarf, gloves for the bus, sunglasses pushed up like a band on her head. Her wallet went into an inside pocket. Trendy enough, but not over-styled. Just confidence in garment form.

Words from this section: outfit, attire, garment, fabric, cotton, wool, denim, leather…

Mini-story

The forecast had threatened a drizzle, but by mid-morning it was a proper downpour. By lunchtime the gust outside the office became a real wind, and the umbrella did its best. Autumn was being autumn. Yesterday had been overcast and chilly; today's sky had thunder, lightning, and the briefest rainbow she'd ever seen. Spring still felt months away. She liked this seasonal English weather, the way it was never the same for two consecutive days — mild in the morning, freezing by 5 p.m., a humid Tuesday followed by a breezy Wednesday and a scorching Saturday. Predictably unpredictable.

Words from this section: drizzle, downpour, shower, hail, blizzard, frost, mist, fog…

Q2.Choose the word that fits: "Germans are stereotypically ____."

deadline
punctual
ongoing
overdue
ongoing (adjective) — still in progress

Mini-story

The deadline was Friday at five, and Friday at five was imminent. The project had been ongoing for months; the recent push felt eventually like a sprint. Meanwhile, two former colleagues had moved on and the upcoming launch needed everyone punctual. She blocked her calendar hourly, marked anything overdue in red, made the prompt replies first, and dealt with subsequently arriving requests in batches. Dawn meetings she avoided; midday calls she tolerated; midnight emails she ignored. Brief, simultaneous, focused — that was the only way to land a temporary push without it becoming permanent burnout.

Words from this section: deadline, punctual, overdue, imminent, ongoing, former, recent, upcoming…

ancestor (noun) — a relative from long ago

Mini-story

At the family reunion last August every relative seemed to have an opinion. Her spouse introduced their toddler to ancestors and elderly aunts. Her teenage nephew sulked in a corner, the adolescent boredom universal. She watched her sibling reconcile with their father after a year of quarrel — a proper argument, fixed by a single hug. Her bond with her mother had always been the loyalty axis of the family; her affection for her grandmother, the warmth. Even the bachelor uncle showed up. The widow next door joined them for supper. Upbringing matters, she thought; you can rely on the people who raised you.

Words from this section: spouse, sibling, relative, offspring, ancestor, descendant, toddler, teenager…

mingle
catch up
hang out
rumour
rumour (noun) — unverified information

Mini-story

The leaving party was small. They mingled, chatted, caught up properly for the first time in months. She hung out with two old acquaintances she barely remembered, was introduced to a new sociable colleague, and listened to a quick toast — cheers, farewell, see you again. The host, ever outgoing, treated everyone to drinks; the reserved guests stayed near the corner. She tried not to gossip but a small rumour was already circulating about who would be promoted next. She apologised for forgetting a name, complimented someone's haircut, and left feeling slightly awkward but very much included.

Words from this section: mingle, catch up, hang out, gossip, rumour, host, guest, toast…

Mini-story

Holding the offer letter, she felt — what was the word? Not just glad. Delighted. Thrilled. Grateful, certainly, for every interview she'd survived. The two months had been long, but she was content with how she'd handled them: hopeful when others doubted her, optimistic when the rejections came, calm in the second-round panel, confident in the final. She felt blissful today, almost ecstatic, but mostly proud — quietly, peacefully proud. Inspired by the new role ahead and amused by how nervous her younger self would have been. A passionate week, a fulfilled month, an upbeat year in the making.

Words from this section: delighted, thrilled, grateful, content, relieved, eager, optimistic, confident…

frustrated

overwhelmed
resentful
jealous (adjective) — fearing rivals in love

Mini-story

The third week of the project was the worst. She felt anxious going into every stand-up, overwhelmed by the backlog, frustrated that the deadline kept moving. By Thursday she was resentful of the manager who'd over-promised, envious of teammates who seemed unbothered, and disappointed in herself for taking it home. She was annoyed at small things — the kettle, the printer, the bus — and irritated at every cheerful e-mail. By Friday night she was furious, miserable, and a bit ashamed of her own reaction. Saturday she felt lonely and uneasy. Then she slept ten hours, woke up, and the gloomy fog had lifted.

Words from this section: overwhelmed, frustrated, resentful, envious, jealous, disappointed, annoyed, irritated…

Mini-story

On paper they were a perfect team: an ambitious lead, a humble engineer, a witty designer with a sarcastic edge, and a tactful project manager who could persuade anyone. Reliable people, all of them — though one was famously stubborn and one famously indecisive. The boss was decisive but a touch arrogant; the deputy was patient but sometimes too cautious. The new joiner turned out to be considerate and compassionate, neither selfish nor reckless. The team had room for both the introverted thinker and the extroverted seller. Honest, modest, generous — when those three traits showed up together, the work got done.

Words from this section: ambitious, modest, humble, arrogant, generous, stingy, dependable, stubborn…

Mini-story

Tuesday morning she had a sore throat and a strange ache behind her eyes. By lunchtime she was nauseous and slightly dizzy. The GP listened to the cough, examined the rash on her arm (itchy, but not swollen), and wrote a prescription. A short course of antibiotics, a stronger painkiller for the headache, and instructions to recover at home for three days. He talked about nutrition, hydrated rest, posture; he reminded her about her flu vaccine. By Friday the symptoms eased, and by the weekend she was back to her usual checkup-fit self.

Words from this section: ache, sore, bruise, swollen, rash, itch, sneeze, cough…

Mini-story

Nights in late October were difficult. She'd doze on the sofa at nine, then lie awake at one. Restless and groggy, she tried everything — a short nap on Saturday, an early bedtime on Sunday, a soft lullaby app, no screens after ten. Her partner snored through her insomnia, yawned cheerfully in the morning, and offered an unhelpful 'just relax'. By Tuesday she was exhausted; by Thursday she was alert again. She unwound with a long walk, recharged with a weekend siesta, woke up refreshed. The nightmare of the previous week — a real, vivid one — had finally passed.

Words from this section: nap, doze, snore, yawn, drowsy, restless, insomnia, nightmare…

flexibility
workout
sprint
jog
flexibility (noun) — ability to bend easily

Mini-story

He'd been meaning to start cardio for months. The treadmill at his gym was old; the dumbbells were chipped; but the workout did its job. He stretched first, jogged for ten minutes, sprinted the last two. After that, yoga for flexibility, a slow plank for endurance, twenty squats for strength, ten crunches for the stomach he'd been ignoring. He swore he could feel his metabolism reboot. Hydrate, calories, muscle, sweat — the four words that explained why he kept coming back. By the cool-down he was tired but fit; by the shower he was thinking about pilates the next day.

Words from this section: workout, jog, sprint, stretch, flexibility, strength, endurance, stamina…

Mini-story

The itinerary was tight: Heathrow at six, layover in Frankfurt, arrival in Singapore by midnight. Departure was on time despite a small delay; her carry-on barely fit in the locker; boarding-pass scanned, customs cleared, currency exchanged at the airport. She'd packed her passport in the front pocket and slipped her visa documents into the same sleeve. Her destination was a small bustling neighbourhood near the river. The first day was for sightseeing — guides, landmarks, scenic alleys. The second was for the remote excursion outside the city. Her baggage arrived safely. Her connecting flight, surprisingly, did not.

Words from this section: itinerary, destination, arrival, layover, boarding, connecting flight, baggage, carry-on…

staycation

Mini-story

The cruise wasn't her usual style. She preferred a quieter retreat — a cabin in the woods, a small camping trip, the kind of hiking that left no Wi-Fi for two days. But this getaway was for her parents. The brochure had promised a complimentary upgrade if you booked early; the reservation included a balcony room; check-in was painless. Day one was snorkelling. Day two was trekking. Day three she used a voucher at the spa. The amenities of the ship were excessive; the rate, surprisingly reasonable. Even jet lag couldn't ruin a staycation at sea, she thought, smiling at the contradiction.

Words from this section: resort, getaway, staycation, retreat, camping, trekking, snorkelling, diving…

Mini-story

Her appraisal at work had finally produced a bonus. After tax it was modest, but the savings account looked healthier than it had in months. She set aside enough for the mortgage, transferred a sum to clear last year's debt, and made a deposit toward the pension she'd been ignoring. The current interest rate made any loan painful, and inflation had quietly eroded her wage. But she had a budget now: track every expense, halve the subscription clutter, claim the unused refund from last month's flight. The invoice for the dentist would go in tomorrow.

Words from this section: savings, budget, expense, income, wage, pension, debt, loan…

Mini-story

The summer sale was already winding down, but she found a real bargain on the clearance rail: a coat with a 40% discount and a five-year warranty. The cashier at the till scanned her loyalty card, applied a voucher she'd forgotten about, and handed her the receipt. The queue behind her was huge but she didn't mind. On the way out she remembered a small exchange she needed to make — wrong size — and the return policy was kind. Retail was exhausting; e-commerce was easier; but a real bricks-and-mortar shop had its own charm.

Words from this section: bargain, discount, clearance, voucher, coupon, trolley, cashier, aisle…

Mini-story

Mondays in the open-plan office were predictable. The intern stood by the photocopier with a stack of paper that wouldn't fit. The supervisor walked past, said hello, and headed to the canteen. A freelancer in the corner cubicle was setting up new equipment. Staff stand-ups at nine, then a quick break before the workforce settled. Sick leave was low this month; absence dropped; annual leave requests were balanced. The dress code was smart-casual, and most flexible hours were respected. Commuting was the only universal complaint. Remote work was the new normal. Some shifts felt longer than others.

Words from this section: supervisor, subordinate, intern, freelancer, contractor, shift, overtime, canteen…

Mini-story

She chaired the Monday review. The agenda had been sent on Friday; the minutes from the previous meeting were attached for any attendee who needed a recap. They kicked off promptly: a brief on customer feedback, a debrief on the launch, and a request to flag any blockers. She asked everyone to follow up offline if needed, looped in the data team via BCC, circled back to a question from last week, and CC'd the broader group on the actions. By the time they wrapped up, half the room had already dialled out for the next call. She RSVP'd to two more meetings before lunch.

Words from this section: attendee, brief, debrief, kick off, wrap up, follow up, circle back, recap…

bandwidth
milestone

bandwidth (noun) — capacity to take on work

Mini-story

The team was halfway through the sprint and the backlog was still alarming. She set a clear milestone for Friday, defined the scope so it wouldn't drift, and asked everyone to flag any blocker before it became a workaround. Bandwidth across the team was tight; the workload was growing; the timeline shifted weekly. She got stakeholder buy-in for a smaller deliverable, aligned with the design lead, delegated two tasks to a colleague, and oversaw the sync. Brainstorms on Friday, KPI review on Monday, a post-mortem after the launch. The roadmap was now a living document — and the team finally felt aligned.

Words from this section: milestone, deliverable, scope, scope creep, bandwidth, workload, backlog, sprint…

CV (noun) — a document listing experience

Mini-story

His appraisal had gone well. The feedback from his mentor had been frank and useful, the KPIs were green, and a quiet promotion conversation had begun. He updated his CV anyway, drafted a careful cover letter for a parallel opportunity, and reached out to networking contacts. After ten years he wasn't ready to resign, but he wanted optionality. The company was hiring two more senior engineers; recent layoffs in the marketing team were still raw; redundancies hadn't reached his floor. The benefits, perks, the commission schedule — everything held together. His career path felt finally his own.

Words from this section: demotion, appraisal, mentee, networking, CV, cover letter, recruit, layoff…

Mini-story

The quarterly review opened with revenue up twelve percent and a record turnover for the scale-up. Profit was healthy; the small loss in Q1 was already absorbed. The investor on the call wanted projections, a clean forecast, and a sense of how growth was being measured. The competitor down the road had cut prices; their market share was creeping up. A possible merger had been floated, and a small acquisition was on the table. The startup was thinking of a pivot, the metrics were good, the strategy held: monetise the new product, hit the KPI, deliver the ROI. The valuation conversation was scheduled for May.

Words from this section: revenue, profit, loss, turnover, growth, forecast, projection, metric…

Mini-story

His charger had broken on Tuesday and his battery was dying by lunchtime. The smartwatch on his wrist showed the same notification every five minutes. He bought new earbuds at the weekend, a wireless charger to replace the cable, and a small touchscreen tablet for reading. The router needed restarting; the Wi-Fi bandwidth was poor when streaming; the storage on his phone was full. He installed the update, ran a backup, changed his password, and turned off the louder notifications. His new login was secure, his old socket finally free. Hardware sorted.

Words from this section: gadget, device, wearable, smartwatch, earbuds, charger, socket, touchscreen…

Mini-story

She'd opened the app to scroll for two minutes and was still scrolling forty later. A meme post about Mondays, three comments on a friend's photo, a livestream from a podcast she subscribed to. Trending audio was unbearable today. A new influencer she'd started to follow had gone viral overnight; another one had been caught in a scam. She blocked the obvious phishing email, marked two messages as spam, and ignored the clickbait at the bottom. The algorithm knew her too well. She closed the tab, opened her blog draft, and wrote two paragraphs without a single hashtag.

Words from this section: browse, scroll, unfollow, hashtag, trending, influencer, subscriber, livestream…

Mini-story

The first lecture of the autumn term was the most intimidating. The curriculum was new, the syllabus dense, and the assignment due next week. She took notes furiously, tried to comprehend the lecturer's accent, and grasped most of it. After the seminar she booked a tutorial with her supervisor and joined a small workshop. The thesis would come later; the essay was due on Friday. She'd start revision in February. A degree, an undergraduate degree, possibly a postgraduate one — she could already see the shape of it. The certificate would arrive in three years; the alumni dinner, in four.

Words from this section: curriculum, syllabus, lecture, seminar, tutorial, assignment, essay, thesis…

Mini-story

The headline broke at seven and by eight the press conference was already trending. A journalist she trusted had pieced together the source; another reporter at a rival paper was in the editorial seat. There was an article on the front page about an outbreak, a scandal at the bank, and a controversy in education policy. Eyewitness statements were scattered across the live coverage; the survivor of the train accident spoke briefly; the minister read a statement. The crisis felt distant for a moment, but the latest update on the ceasefire reminded her how thin the rope was.

Words from this section: headline, breaking news, editorial, reporter, coverage, rumour, scandal, controversy…

Mini-story

The election campaign had grown quieter in its final week. A candidate from the opposition had pulled ahead; the government was on the defensive; the coalition partners were already manoeuvring. Policy debates filled every evening; a bill on welfare was stalled; a vote on immigration legislation was scheduled. Civil rights groups marched in a peaceful protest. Across the border, refugees were being processed. Inequality was the word of the week; equality was on every leaflet. The citizen who'd never voted before was suddenly informed. Tax, nationality, majority — the country argued in three weeks what it usually argued in three years.

Words from this section: policy, legislation, candidate, opposition, coalition, minority, democracy, dictatorship…

Mini-story

The wildfire that had raged for two weeks had finally been contained, but the damage to the habitat was extensive. Biodiversity in the region was already fragile; the species count had fallen; two endangered birds might soon be extinct. Climate models were predicting more drought, more flood, more chaos. Emissions in the country had grown despite the policy on renewable energy; solar and wind power were rising but fossil-fuel use lingered. Recycling was mainstream now; sustainable supply chains were the new ambition. Her carbon footprint, like everyone else's, needed sustained effort, not gestures.

Words from this section: climate, global warming, greenhouse gas, emissions, carbon footprint, renewable, solar, wind power…

crime (noun) — an illegal act
Q4.Choose the word that fits: "The ____ escaped on foot."
illegal
law
crime
criminal
criminal (noun) — a person committing crime
Q5.Choose the word that fits: "A minor traffic ____."
illegal
law
offence
crime
offence (noun) — a breaking of the law

Mini-story

The verdict came back on a Wednesday. The trial had lasted six weeks. The lawyer had been patient with the witness; the evidence had been clear; the suspect was not the criminal everyone had assumed. The jury was unanimous: not guilty. There would be no jail, no fine, no sentence — but an appeal was already being drafted by the prosecution. The judge thanked the room and closed the case. Two journalists waited outside. A small offence had started this; a burglary had ended it. Legal systems, she thought, mostly worked. Mostly.

Words from this section: law, illegal, crime, criminal, offence, suspect, witness, trial…

Mini-story

The two of them had been arguing for an hour. He claimed the data was decisive; she asserted the data was flawed. He insisted; she conceded one small point but disputed the rest. She refused to deny her opposition; he refused to acknowledge the limits of his own reasoning. Each tried to convince, persuade, debate the other into agreement. By eleven the logic had collapsed into perspective. He admitted his assumption was strong; she recognised her conclusion was incomplete. They didn't agree, but they understood each other better. A civil contradiction, justified and reasonable.

Words from this section: assert, insist, admit, concede, disagree, oppose, convince, persuade…

Mini-story

The presentation went well. The product was elegant; however, the pricing strategy needed work. Sales had grown; therefore the team could afford to invest. Moreover, the customer feedback was strong, furthermore the technical reviews glowing. Nevertheless, the launch still carried risk. Whereas competitors had gone broad, the company had gone deep; consequently the market understood the value. Although the timeline was tight, the team delivered. Despite the delays, the result held up. Hence the celebration; thus the relief. Firstly, gratitude; secondly, action; finally, the next chapter. Overall — and ultimately — a year that justified itself.

Words from this section: however, therefore, moreover, furthermore, nevertheless, nonetheless, whereas, although…

Mini-story

Arguably the best decision of his career was the boring one. He'd been presumably tired, apparently uncertain, seemingly without a plan. Allegedly his manager had warned him. Essentially the choice came down to two paths, and fundamentally he picked the safer one. Primarily because the timing was right; largely because the team needed stability; predominantly because his own bandwidth was thin. Partially out of caution, relatively low ambition, approximately right but not heroic. Somewhat conservative, rather safe, fairly ordinary. Quite a sensible move. Considerably less glamorous than the alternative; substantially more sustainable. He'd tend to make the same call again. It might just have been the right move.

Words from this section: arguably, presumably, apparently, seemingly, supposedly, allegedly, essentially, fundamentally…

Mini-story

The pitch was excellent; the prototype was outstanding; the demo was superb. The team's engineering was impressive, the remarkable thing was how quickly they'd built it. The decision to ship was decisive, not impressive — just clearly right. The product itself was robust, the data flow flawless, the architecture sophisticated. Their previous version had been dreadful, almost appalling. They'd called the old code substandard and they'd been honest about it. The compelling thing was the new plan: convincing data, a plausible roadmap, a worthwhile bet. Not flawless, not without dubious assumptions, but notable. Definitely not mediocre. Definitely not pointless.

Words from this section: outstanding, superb, impressive, remarkable, decent, mediocre, dreadful, appalling…

Mini-story

The shop was rarely busy on Tuesdays — hardly ever, in fact. Occasionally a regular dropped in; frequently the same three customers in the afternoon. Plenty of stock, a handful of best-sellers, scarce supply of one obscure cheese. Numerous brands competed for shelf space, but countless small producers were being squeezed. The majority of buyers were local; a minority drove in from the next village. Ample evidence suggested the shop should open later; sparse data suggested the opposite. Seldom did the owner make a decision quickly. Regularly he opened at seven, perpetually he closed at six. The rhythm was steady, intermittent only on Sundays — widespread habit, occasional change.

Words from this section: plenty, scarce, abundant, ample, sparse, dense, numerous, countless…

Mini-story

Tech shares opened poorly, dipped at noon, plummeted by close. Then the index recovered. By Tuesday morning the line had stabilised; by Wednesday it had soared. Volatility had transformed the week — the market fluctuated like a heartbeat. One stock surged ninety percent; another plunged forty. The company decelerated its hiring; another accelerated its expansion plans. Old industries shrank, new ones expanded; some habits evolved, some adapted, some simply collapsed. The world had shifted — a quiet transition that no one noticed until it was over. By Friday the market had reversed the worst of the damage; the boost was real; the decline, hopefully, behind them.

Words from this section: decrease, rise, plummet, soar, plunge, surge, dip, stabilise…
39

Mini-story

He mumbled an apology, sighed at his own bad reception, and whispered to his colleague that he'd missed half the call. Someone yelled at a kid in the next room; the kid screamed back; the parent groaned. Across the open-plan office, a manager muttered something about a report and chuckled at her own joke. Another colleague kept nagging the IT team. A junior engineer bragged about a fix; another boasted about a refactor; the lead complimented both and warned them not to flatter each other. The HR manager scolded no one, reminded everyone, suggested a fix. He explained, clarified, didn't interrupt, responded promptly, and tried not to misinterpret a single thing.

Words from this section: mumble, whisper, yell, scream, chuckle, giggle, sigh, groan…
40

Mini-story

He'd been in the UK three months now. The flat was small, the lift always broken, the queue at the bus stop a daily reality. He bought petrol at the local petrol station, parked in a tiny car park, walked the pavement to the high street. His trousers were too long, his jumper too thin, his trainers fine. He'd discovered crisps were not chips, chips were not fries, and a biscuit was not what an American called a biscuit. He took the Tube, paid the post, sorted the rubbish into the right dustbin, and finally figured out the ground floor was not the first floor. 'Cheers, mate,' he said. 'Brilliant.'

Words from this section: lift, queue up, lorry, petrol, petrol station, car park, pavement, zebra crossing…
41

Quick check — Native Collocations (replace Turkish calques)

Pick the word that fits each gap. Click an option for instant feedback.
Q1.Choose the word that fits: "She had to ____ quickly."
make progress
make a plan
make a mistake
make a decision
make a decision (phrase) — decide (NOT 'give a decision')
Q2.Choose the word that fits: "Let's ____ for the weekend."
make progress
make a plan
make a mistake
make a decision
make a plan (phrase) — plan (NOT 'do a plan')
Q3.Choose the word that fits: "Everyone makes mistakes."
make progress
make a plan
make a mistake
make a decision
make a mistake (phrase) — do something wrong (NOT 'do a mistake')
Q4.Choose the word that fits: "We're making real progress now."
make progress
make a plan
make a mistake
make a decision
make progress (phrase) — advance (NOT 'do progress')
Q5.Choose the word that fits: "Make an effort to be on time."
make an effort
make a plan
make a mistake
make a decision
make an effort (phrase) — try hard (NOT 'do an effort')

Mini-story

She had a decision to make, a plan to put together, and a mistake from last quarter to fix. She decided to do it properly this time: make a list, take a chance on the new approach, make an effort to involve the team. She wouldn't 'give' a decision the way she would in Turkish; she'd make one. She wouldn't 'do' a party for the launch; she'd throw one. She wouldn't 'make' sport; she'd do exercise. She'd take advantage of the spare hour, take a break when she needed one, take care of the small details. By the end of the day she'd made sense of the chaos and made real progress.

Words from this section: make a decision, make a plan, make a mistake, make progress, make an effort, make sense, make money, throw a party…

Quick check — C1 Opinion Frames & Discourse

Pick the word that fits each gap. Click an option for instant feedback.
Q1.Choose the word that fits: "In my view, the policy is sound."
in my view
what strikes me is

Q2.Choose the word that fits: "From my perspective, it's worth it."
in my view
what strikes me is
from my perspective
I would argue

what strikes me is
from my perspective

Q4.Choose the word that fits: "What strikes me is the silence."
in my view
what strikes me is

Q5.Choose the word that fits: "On balance, I'd say yes."
in my view
on balance
from my perspective
I would argue
on balance (phrase) — considering everything

Mini-story

When she gave her opinion in meetings now, she sounded different. 'In my view,' she'd say, not 'I think maybe'. 'From my perspective,' not 'kind of like'. 'I would argue,' not 'so it is'. What strikes me, she'd add, is the cost. On balance, the data supports the move. To some extent, the risk is manageable. By and large, the team agrees. On the whole, this is the right call. There's no denying the deadline is tight. It's worth noting the dependency. Having said that, the upside is real. To put it simply: we should go. To sum up: now. In conclusion: yes. All things considered, the answer is obvious.

Words from this section: in my view, from my perspective, I would argue, what strikes me is, on balance, to some extent, by and large, on the whole…

Q1.Choose the word that fits: "Sort out the printer issue."

Q2.Choose the word that fits: "Follow up after the meeting."

Q3.Choose the word that fits: "We ____ the project Monday."

kick off
follow up

sort out
look into (phrasal verb) — investigate

Mini-story

By Tuesday she'd sorted out the printer, followed up on the unanswered emails, and kicked off the new project. She looked into the customer complaint, ran the idea by her manager, and pushed back gently when the deadline slipped. She caught up with two team-mates and made sure no one fell behind. The senior dev was taking over the platform; the lead was handing over the contract; the director had to sign off the changes. She logged in early, logged out late, drew up a fresh proposal, came up with three names for the launch, and carried out the audit on time. By Friday they wrapped up, went over the slides one final time, and touched on the next quarter.

Words from this section: sort out, follow up, kick off, wrap up, look into, run by, push back, catch up…

dress up (phrasal verb) — wear smart clothes

Mini-story

She got up at six, woke up the kids by quarter past, and dressed up the youngest for school. They ate out for breakfast at the corner café (a small treat), then she dropped off the kids and turned on the radio. After work she picked up the parcel from the post office, called back her sister, and figured out the weekend plans. She turned down the heating, took off her shoes, put on her slippers. The kids showed up at five, ran out of patience by six, and cheered up after dinner. By nine the day had ended. By ten she'd already come across tomorrow's first email.

Words from this section: get up, wake up, eat out, dine in, dress up, hang out, turn on, turn off…

Quick check — Common Idioms

Pick the word that fits each gap. Click an option for instant feedback.
Q1.Choose the word that fits: "The test was a ____."
piece of cake
hit the books
under the weather
break the ice
piece of cake (idiom) — very easy
Q2.Choose the word that fits: "I need to ____ tonight."
piece of cake
break the ice
hit the books
under the weather
hit the books (idiom) — study hard
Q3.Choose the word that fits: "I'm a bit ____."
piece of cake
hit the books
under the weather
break the ice
under the weather (idiom) — feeling unwell
Q4.Choose the word that fits: "A joke helps ____."
piece of cake
hit the books
under the weather
break the ice
break the ice (idiom) — start a conversation
Q5.Choose the word that fits: "That car ____."
piece of cake
hit the books
cost an arm and a leg
under the weather
cost an arm and a leg (idiom) — very expensive

Mini-story

The test had been a piece of cake; he'd hit the books for two weeks and the questions felt easy. But by mid-afternoon he was under the weather, his head was pounding, and he was ready to call it a day. He'd burnt the midnight oil three nights in a row. The job offer, when it came, was a blessing in disguise — a better company than he'd hoped, though it cost an arm and a leg in relocation. 'Speak of the devil,' his manager said when he saw him. They were finally on the same page. The ball was in his court now, and once in a blue moon, he was the one to bite the bullet first.

Words from this section: piece of cake, hit the books, under the weather, break the ice, cost an arm and a leg, once in a blue moon, bite the bullet, call it a day…
46

stationery
advise
complement
compliment
stationery (noun) — writing supplies

Mini-story

The new policy would affect every team. Its effect on morale was already visible. She wanted to advise her colleagues, but her advice felt out of date. The dish complemented the wine; her boss complimented the chef. The principal reason for the change was a deep principle of fairness. The stationery cupboard was empty; the stationary car outside the window had been there for hours. Her trousers were loose; she didn't want to lose another pair. She'd accept all the feedback except the one about her accent. A personal note from the personnel manager arrived, and the device she'd been asked to devise was still in her head. She took a deep breath, breathed out, and got on with it.

Words from this section: advise, complement, compliment, principle, stationery, stationary, personal, personnel…
47

Mini-story

He nodded as she spoke, shrugged when she asked for opinions, and frowned at the costs. She grinned at his honesty, smirked at his cynicism, and winked once when he wasn't looking. Across the table, a colleague stared at his laptop while another glanced at the clock and a third glared at the messy whiteboard. He blinked twice, clenched his fist, waved off the criticism, and clapped politely at the end. The new joiner blushed when introduced, squinted at the slides, gasped at the budget, shivered when the heating broke. He hesitated at the door, flinched at the bad coffee, cringed at the joke, and beamed when the meeting ended.

Words from this section: nod, shrug, frown, grin, smirk, wink, stare, glance…

Mini-story

The bistro near the river was a small joy. The starters were generous, the appetiser she picked unforgettable, the main course glorious. They split a side dish, ordered two desserts, and the specials of the day were exactly what she needed. The vegetarian option was lovely. The waiter was attentive; the chef came out to say hello; the menu was bilingual. The tip she left was generous, the bill they split was fair. Her reservation had been worth the month's wait. Hearty, rich, light, savoury — every adjective on the menu, none of it greasy or overdone. The ambience felt cosy without being precious.

Words from this section: starter, appetiser, main course, side dish, dessert, specials, vegetarian option, tip…

Mini-story

The new concept was difficult to grasp. The notion that two opposing theories could both be right struck her as a paradigm she'd been missing. She made a hypothesis, tested an assumption, sat with her belief and her doubt. Conviction without certainty, intuition with insight — the awareness slowly came. Perception, realisation, reflection. She'd contemplated the dilemma all week and pondered it again on Saturday. She didn't want to speculate, but she could anticipate the answer. The rationale was clear; the trade-off was real; uncertainty was simply the price of any predict-able future.

Words from this section: notion, hypothesis, assumption, belief, conviction, doubt, uncertainty, certainty…
